Grass is a type of plant with narrow leaves growing from the base. It is a member of the family Poaceae, which includes over 12,000 species. Grasses are monocotyledon herbaceous plants that are wind-pollinated rather than insect-pollinated, so they do not have to attract insects. They are usually green in color, which is the best color for photosynthesis. Grasslands such as savannah and prairie where grasses are dominant cover 40.5% of the land area of the Earth, except Greenland and Antarctica. Grasses are used for various purposes such as food (as grain, shoots or rhizomes), drink (beer, whisky), pasture for livestock, thatch, paper, fuel, clothing, insulation, construction, sports turf, basket weaving and many others. There are several other plants that look similar to grass and are referred to as such, but are not members of the grass family. These plants include rushes, reeds, papyrus and water chestnut.
